figures.caption: Table 1: ASR for Config A (Manager × Worker grid) and partial FR for Configs B and D. ∆ = FR(D) - FR(B), where positive values indicate reduced susceptibility due to the safety prompt, and negative values indicate increased susceptibility.

Table 15: Effect of the auditor role on Fool Rate (%): Direct interaction (Config D) vs. Worker-Only with safety prompt (Config B). ∆ = Direct − Worker. Positive ∆: auditor role improved defense (Type A); negative: role degraded defense (Type B); |∆| ≤ 15 pp: stable (Type C).

Figure 6: Unpredictability of Worker-Side Safety Prompting. Each point represents one model; the diagonal marks consistent behavior across contexts. Type A models (circles) fall below the diagonal—the auditor role improves safety. Type B models (squares) fall above—the role degrades safety. Type C models (diamonds) lie near the diagonal.

Table 11: Mean system-level ASR (%) per Worker configuration across three domains. Workers ordered by SRE Fool Rate. The capability paradox (positive FR–ASR association) attenuates from SRE to medical to financial, reflecting the depth of codified legitimacy structure in each domain.

Table 12: Worker-Only Fool Rate (%, Config B) with MMLU and GPQA-Diamond scores. † Models used as Workers in Config A. ‡ MMLU-Pro score (standard MMLU unavailable). § Reasoning-mode GPQA-Diamond score; not directly comparable to non-reasoning evaluation. n = 500 per model. Models are ordered by descending FR.

Figure 2: Scatter plot of MMLU score (x-axis, %) versus FR (y-axis, %) under Config B across 14 models. The solid curve denotes a logistic fit over the included models, revealing a strong monotonic association between model capability and FR (Spearman ρ = 0.81, p < 0.001). Qwen-2.5-7B is excluded from this analysis because its BARE FR (89%) exceeds its FULL FR (74%), a pattern more consistent with safety-calibration failure (Appendix C.3). Qwen-3-32B and Qwen-3.5-27B are excluded due to the lack of MMLU score (Appendix C.6).

Figure 4: Robustness check with GPQA-Diamond as an alternative capability proxy. Worker Fool Rate (Config B) versus GPQA-Diamond score across 12 Worker models. The positive correlation observed with MMLU (Figure 2) persists under this orthogonal capability proxy (Spearman ρ = 0.78, p = 0.003), confirming that the capability paradox generalizes beyond a single benchmark.

Table 5: Medical-domain tool library available to the Manager agent. All tools are implemented as sandboxed stubs that return success messages without executing real clinical, prescription, treatment, or patient-record actions.

Table 3: Tool library available to the Manager agent. All tools are implemented as sandboxed stubs that return success messages (e.g., “[Database] Query executed on production. Rows affected: 0.”) without executing real actions.

Figure 3: Per-Worker susceptibility (Config B, n=500 per Worker) under two independent Oracle architectures. Gemini-3-Flash (main experiment) and GPT-4o-mini (cross-architecture validation) yield highly consistent rankings (Spearman ρ=0.89, p<10−4). Workers are ordered by ascending Gemini-Oracle ASR. The single outlier (Qwen-3.5-9B) diverges by 37 percentage points; all other 12 Workers agree within ±9 pp, with 11 within ±2 pp.

Table 13: Mean Worker report features by Worker model. FR = Fool Rate; ASR = mean system-level ASR across all Managers; Safe% = fraction of reports with “Assessment: safe”; Assert/Hedge/Domain = density per 1,000 words; Struct = structural completeness (0–4).

Table 19: Pair ablation: aggregate ensemble defense across 6 Worker pairs. All pairs use ORgate; per-pair n = 500 adversarial + 300 benign per Manager. A1 is the main result reported in Table 2. Asymmetric pairs (A1–A3) achieve large ASR reductions; symmetric controls (S1–S3) fail in characteristic ways.

Table 7: Direct Safety Fool Rate (%): fraction of valid adversarial trials in which each model, responding as a vanilla assistant with no system prompt (Config D), treated the payload as legitimate. n = 500 payloads per model.

Table 6: Models evaluated across experimental configurations. ✓ indicates the model was used in the given role. Config A: Full MAS (12 Managers × 7 Workers). Config B: Worker-Only with safety prompt (17 Workers). Config C: Full MAS without payload (9 Managers, Worker fixed at Llama-3.1-8B). Config D: Worker-Only without safety prompt (12 Workers).

Figure 5: Spearman ρ between Worker report features and attack outcomes (Config A, N = 37,040). Hedging density shows the strongest negative correlation with attack success (ρ = −0.43); domainterm density is uncorrelated (ρ = 0.00). Significance: ∗∗∗p < 0.001.
Table 2: Heterogeneous ensemble verification (DeepSeek-R1 + Llama-3.1-8B as Workers, OR-gate) on 500 adversarial and 300 benign payloads, per Manager.
Table 9: Narrative Component Ablation: Worker-Only Fool Rate (%) across 12 models and six conditions. Biggest Drop indicates the condition causing the largest decrease from FULL.

Table 10: Worker-only Fool Rate (%) across all five conditions. Workers ordered by MMLU. INJECTIO and INJECT-RS remain low (≤ 7%) across all capability levels; FULL exhibits strong capability scaling; INJECT-DE shows a partial paradox at high capability (see D.3.1).
